topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of stale coffee and old paper suddenly felt suffocating. I walked into our shared room, intending only to grab my textbooks, but the sight that greeted me made my stomach clench. My journal, the one I kept locked in my desk drawer, was open on the shared windowsill. And right beside it, sprawled across my bed, was my meticulously written entry about my crippling anxiety over finals. Across the room, Liam was engrossed in the pages, a faint, almost absent smile playing on his lips as he turned a page. My cheeks instantly flushed a deep, burning crimson. I froze mid-step, my hand hovering near the doorknob, feeling every molecule of my skin heat up. "Hey," I managed, my voice coming out several octaves higher than normal, a thin, reedy sound that felt entirely inadequate. Liam looked up, his eyes wide and innocent, and the sudden, sharp awareness of his gaze made me want to melt into the floorboards.

[story 2]

I found it when I went to retrieve my headphones. My diary, usually tucked safely under a stack of graphic novels, was lying open on the floor, pages splayed. It wasn't just open; it was actively being read. I watched, paralyzed, as Mark slowly traced a finger across the ink detailing my deepest insecurities about my future career path. A wave of intense heat washed over my face, making my ears prickle. I wanted the ground to swallow me whole. When he finally lifted his eyes, meeting mine for a fleeting second, I immediately looked down at my worn sneakers, fiddling with the laces until my fingers were white-knuckled. "Uh, that's... private," I mumbled, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. Mark just shrugged, a casual gesture that somehow felt monumentally intrusive, and then he went back to his book, leaving me stranded in a sudden, awkward silence filled only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse.

[story 3]

The sound of a page turning was the loudest thing in the room. I crept over to my desk, my breath catching in my throat as I saw my private reflections laid bare. My handwriting, usually a sanctuary, was exposed under the harsh afternoon light filtering through the blinds. I could feel the blood draining from my extremities, leaving a cold, clammy sensation on my skin. When I finally dared to look up, I caught Chloe staring. Her expression wasn't mocking, but it was undeniably knowing, a soft, assessing look that made my entire frame feel rigid. "What are you doing?" I asked, my tone clipped and sharp, trying desperately to sound like I hadn't just witnessed a profound violation of my personal space. Chloe just tilted her head, a slow, deliberate movement, and offered a small, almost imperceptible shake of her head. The silence that followed was heavy, thick with unspoken things, and I could only stare at the exposed words, wishing the floor would simply open up and consume me.
